Student Exam Performance Dataset Analysis

This project is to analyse how the various student-related academic, lifestyle, and socio-enconmic factores influence the exam perdormance and identify the patterns and relationships between these factors and the student's score.The dataset include study hours,student attendence,sleep hours,motivation,tutoring sessions,parental involvement,school type and other envirmental features.The main target varibale is Exam_Score which represents the student final exam score.
In [1]:
#importing libraries
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
#Setting the background style of the plot
sns.set_style("whitegrid")

The attributes to be analysed are Hours_Studied, Attendance,Parental_Involvement,Access_to_Resources, Extracurricular_Activities, Sleep_Hours,Previous_Scores,Motivation_Level,Internet_Access,Tutoring_Sessions,Family_Income,Teacher_Quality, School_Type,Peer_Influence,Physical_Activity, Learning_Disabilities,Parental_Education_Level,Distance_from_Home,Gender and Exam_Score

In [9]:
#Handling missing values
#Since it is a categorical column,selecting the top most common value to fill null values
df["Teacher_Quality"]=df["Teacher_Quality"].fillna(df["Teacher_Quality"].mode()[0])
In [10]:
#Since it is a categorical column,selecting the top most common value to fill null values
df["Parental_Education_Level"]=df["Parental_Education_Level"].fillna(df["Parental_Education_Level"].mode()[0])

df["Distance_from_Home"]=df["Distance_from_Home"].fillna(df["Distance_from_Home"].mode()[0])

In [13]:
#Formatting
#creating a list of categorical columns and changing the datatype as category
categorical_cols=["Parental_Involvement","Access_to_Resources","Extracurricular_Activities","Motivation_Level","Internet_Access","Family_Income","Teacher_Quality","School_Type","Peer_Influence","Learning_Disabilities","Parental_Education_Level","Distance_from_Home","Gender"]
for col in categorical_cols:
    df[col]=df[col].astype("category")

In [23]:
#Derived features
#Adding a new column to the DataFrame
df["Average_score"]=(df["Previous_Scores"]+df["Exam_Score"])/2
In [24]:
#Assigning grades by using function
def assign_grade(score) :
    if score>=90:
        return "A"
    elif score>=75:
        return "B"
    elif score>=60:
        return "c"
    elif score>=45:
        return "D"
    else :
        return "E"
df["Grade"]=df["Exam_Score"].apply(assign_grade)
In [25]:
#Transformation- Normalizing Attendance
df["Attendance_norm"]=((df["Attendance"])-df["Attendance"].min())/(df["Attendance"].max()-df["Attendance"].min())
df["Attendance_norm"]=df["Attendance_norm"].round(1)
df["Attendance_norm"]

EDA & Visualizations
In [30]:
#Visualization 1
#univariate analysis
#To change the figure size
plt.figure(figsize=(8,6))
#creating histogram with green bars
sns.histplot(df["Exam_Score"],bins=20,kde=True,color="green",edgecolor="black")
#Giving title of the plot
plt.title("Distribution of Exam_Score",color="green",fontsize=14)
#Giving x label and y label
plt.xlabel("Exam_Score",color="green",fontsize=12)
plt.ylabel("count",color="green",fontsize=12)
#Giving color and fontsize to xticks and yticks
plt.xticks(color="green",fontsize=8)
plt.yticks(color="green",fontsize=8)
#To show the plot
plt.show()

The Histogram - Distribution of Exam_score portraits that most students scored in between 60 and 70

In [31]:
#Visualization 2
#Gender distribution
plt.figure(figsize=(8,6))
sns.countplot(x="Gender",data=df)
plt.title("Gender distribution",color="blue",fontsize=14)
plt.xlabel("Gender",color="blue",fontsize=12)
plt.ylabel("count",color="blue",fontsize=12)
plt.xticks(color="blue",fontsize=8)
plt.yticks(color="blue",fontsize=8)
plt.show()

The countplot shows that male students are more numerous than females students

In [32]:
#Visualization 3
#Bivariate analysis
#Scatter plot
plt.figure(figsize=(8,6))
sns.scatterplot(x="Hours_Studied",y="Exam_Score",data=df,hue="Gender")
plt.title("Hours_Studied vs Exam_Score",color="blue",fontsize=14)
plt.xlabel("Hours_Studied",color="blue",fontsize=12)
plt.ylabel("Exam_Score",color="blue",fontsize=12)
plt.xticks(color="blue",fontsize=8)
plt.yticks(color="blue",fontsize=8)
plt.legend(title="Gender",title_fontsize=10, fontsize=8,loc="upper left",frameon=True,edgecolor="black")
plt.show()

The Scatter plot-Hours_Studied vs Exam_Score indicates that the female students who scored high marks studied 10-20 hours whereas the male students with high scores studied 20-30 hours.This suggest that female students grasp faster than male students.It highlights the differrence in the study efficiency among genders in the given dataset.

In [33]:
#Visualization 4
#Barplot
#creating a new column and dividing the attendence into intervals
df["Attendance_bin"]=pd.cut(df["Attendance"],bins=5)
plt.figure(figsize=(8,6))
sns.barplot(x="Attendance_bin",y="Exam_Score",data=df,color="purple")
plt.title("Attendance vs Exam_Score",color="purple",fontsize=14)
plt.xlabel("Attendance",color="purple",fontsize=12)
plt.ylabel("Exam_Score",color="purple",fontsize=12)
plt.xticks(color="purple",fontsize=8)
plt.yticks(color="purple",fontsize=8)
plt.show()

The Barplot indicates that the students with maximum attendance scored high marks,which implies attendence in an influencing factor in scoring high marks

In [34]:
#Visualization 5
#Line plot
plt.figure(figsize=(8,6))
sns.lineplot(x="Sleep_Hours",y="Exam_Score",data=df,color="red",linewidth=2)
plt.title("Sleep_Hours vs Exam_Score",color="red",fontsize=14)
plt.xlabel("Sleep_Hours",color="red",fontsize=12)
plt.ylabel("Exam_Score",color="red",fontsize=12)
plt.xticks(color="red",fontsize=8)
plt.yticks(color="red",fontsize=8)
plt.show()

The above linegraph portraits that the students sleep more scored less marks,instead those who sleep less have scored more.Sleep_hour is also an affecting factor in scoring marks

In [35]:
#Visualization 6
#Multivariate analysis
#Heatmap
plt.figure(figsize=(8,6))
#calculating correlation between numerical columns ,showing values in each column and choosing the color style
sns.heatmap(df.corr(numeric_only=True), annot=True, cmap="viridis",linecolor="black")
plt.title("Correlation Heatmap of Numerical Features",color="darkblue",fontsize=14)
plt.show()

The correlation heatmap indicates how the numerical values are related .The value ranges from -1 to 1,1 indicates both increases together and -1 indicates inverse relationship and 0 means no linear relationship

In [36]:
#Visualization 7
#Boxplot
plt.figure(figsize=(8,6))
sns.boxplot(x="Parental_Education_Level",y="Exam_Score",hue="Gender",data=df,)
plt.title("Exam score by Parental_Education_Level and Gender",color="brown",fontsize=14)
plt.xlabel("Parental_Education_Level",color="brown",fontsize=12)
plt.ylabel("Exam_Score",color="brown",fontsize=12)
plt.xticks(color="brown",fontsize=8)
plt.yticks(color="brown",fontsize=8)
#showing legend on the plot
plt.legend(title="Gender",title_fontsize=10, fontsize=8,loc="upper right",frameon=True,edgecolor="black")
plt.show()

The boxplot-Exam score by Parental_Education_Level and Gender reveals that in all parental_Education_Levels female students scored high marks and also most students have high scores with High school Parental_Education_Level, which implies the Parental_Education_Level dosen't influence to score more.

In [37]:
#Visualization 8
#Pairplot
plt.figure(figsize=(10,8))
sns.pairplot(df[["Hours_Studied","Attendance","Previous_Scores","Exam_Score"]],kind="scatter",diag_kind="kde")
plt.xticks(color="blue",fontsize=8)
plt.yticks(color="blue",fontsize=8)
plt.show() 

<Figure size 1000x800 with 0 Axes>

The above pairplot visualize the relationship between Hours_Studied,Attendance,Previous_Scores and Exam_Score

In [38]:
#Visualization 9
#Barplot
plt.figure(figsize=(8,6))
df["Previous_Scores_bin"] = pd.cut(df['Previous_Scores'], bins=5)
sns.barplot(x="Previous_Scores_bin",y="Exam_Score",hue="Internet_Access",data=df,palette={"Yes":"green", "No":"red"})
plt.title("Exam Score and Previous Scores vs Internet Access",color="red",fontsize=14)
plt.xlabel("Previous Scores",color="red",fontsize=12)
plt.ylabel("Exam Score",color="green",fontsize=12)
plt.xticks(color="red",fontsize=8,rotation=45)
plt.yticks(color="green",fontsize=8)
plt.legend(title="Internet_Access",title_fontsize=10, fontsize=8,loc="lower left",frameon=True,edgecolor="black")
plt.show()

The Bargraph-Exam Score and Previous Scores vs Internet Access Shows that the students with Internet access have scored high marks than others.That implies accessing internet for studies is helpful for scoring marks according to this dataset.

In [39]:
#Visualization 10
#Boxplot
plt.figure(figsize=(10,8))
sns.boxplot(x="Teacher_Quality", y="Exam_Score", data=df,color="yellow")
plt.title("Exam score by Teacher quality",color="darkblue",fontsize=14)
plt.xlabel("Teacher_Quality",color="darkblue",fontsize=12)
plt.ylabel("Exam_Score",color="darkblue",fontsize=12)
plt.xticks(color="darkblue",fontsize=8)
plt.yticks(color="darkblue",fontsize=8)
plt.show()

This bivariate boxplot show three types of teacher quality(high,low,medium)and their influence in the Exam score of students.The plot shows that the teachers with medium quality helps students to score high and the low quality teachers are not much beneficial for students to score high marks.At the same time high quality teachers influences in a moderate way

In [40]:
#Visualization 11
#Piechart
#counting how many times each grade occurs
grade_count=df["Grade"].value_counts().sort_index()
plt.figure(figsize=(8,6))
plt.pie(grade_count,labels=grade_count.index,colors=["green","red","orange","blue","yellow"],startangle=90)
plt.axis("equal")
plt.title("Overview of Grades", fontsize=16, color="green")
plt.show()

The piechart-Grades shows that the majory of students achieve the C grade and only a few students shows their excellence in achieving A grade.This give an insight that various student-related academic, lifestyle, and socio-enconmic factores influence the efficiency to study in one way or in another way
